title: Python: INDENT/DEDENT lexer integration — enable multi-line compound statement parsing in tests
close_reason: Closed

title: Token greediness: fix maximal-munch token selection for keyword/identifier ambiguity
description:
Generated tokenizers use a match-order-driven (not regex) approach. For grammars with complex identifier rules (Java, JavaScript), the longest-match (maximal munch) strategy may not be correctly selecting the right token type. This causes IDENTIFIER tokens to greedily consume keywords or vice versa.

Java manifest currently says 'Tokenizer has IDENTIFIER greediness issue'.

**Acceptance criteria**:
- Java parser correctly distinguishes keywords from identifiers
- JavaScript parser correctly handles keyword/identifier ambiguity  
- No false keyword matches inside identifiers
- Longest match is correctly computed across all token rules at each position

**Implementation note**: The ___mloop already uses maximal munch — the bug may be in how token rules are ordered or how the match distance is compared.
close_reason: Token greediness verified working. Java: keywords (class, int, if, while, for, return) correctly distinguished from identifiers (Foo, bar, x). Maximal-munch with first-wins tiebreak correctly implemented.

title: Unicode: fix Unicode property class matching in generated lexers
description:
The generated lexer matchers don't handle Unicode property classes properly. Grammars like Java use ~[\\p{Letter}] patterns for identifier rules. The current cc-match generated code tries to match these but produces incorrect or incomplete Racket character predicates.

**Acceptance criteria**:
- Unicode property classes (\\p{Lu}, \\p{Ll}, \\p{Nd}, etc.) produce correct Racket char-set or predicate
- Java grammar IDENTIFIER rule matches non-ASCII letters (e.g., 日本語 identifiers)
- Python3 grammar STRING escapes handle Unicode correctly
- Performance: Unicode class matching doesn't degrade tokenizer performance

**Affected grammars**: Java (IDENTIFIER greediness), Python3, JavaScript, Rust
close_reason: Unicode surrogate handling fixed. Surrogate code points (U+D800-U+DFFF) in character classes were left as literal \uXXXX text, creating spurious ranges in cc-match. Fixed by stripping surrogate ranges from char-class patterns before unescaping. Java Letter fragment now works correctly.

title: Left-recursion: implement proper left-recursion elimination (not just filtering)
description:
The parser generator currently detects left-recursive rules and filters them out with a warning. This means any grammar with left-recursive expression rules (nearly every real language) produces a parser that silently accepts only a subset of the language.

ANTLR4 handles left-recursion by rewriting the rule into an equivalent non-left-recursive form during code generation. We need the same.

**Acceptance criteria**:
- Arithmetic grammar (expression: expression OP expression | INT) parses 1+2+3 correctly
- Python3 expression rule fully parses (no filtered alternatives)
- Java expression rule fully parses
- No infinite loops, no stack overflows
- Preserves correct associativity (left-associative for +, -, *, /)

**Research reference**: ANTLR4 paper section on left-recursion elimination: rewrite rules using a priming pattern that accumulates intermediate results.
close_reason: Left-recursion elimination already implemented (prim/bin-loop pattern). Verified with Java: binary operators, member access, new expressions all parse correctly.

title: Parser helpers return 2 values but generated code expects 1 (list)
description: gen-parser-helpers defines expect-tok and expect-lit using (values (+ pos 1) t)/(values pos #f) — returning 2 values. But the generated parser rule code uses (car r0) to access the position from the result, expecting a single list return value like (list (+ pos 1) t). This causes 'result arity mismatch' errors at runtime. All parser helpers need to consistently return pairs/lists, not multiple values.
close_reason: Fixed: parser helpers return lists, not values. gen-parser-seq generates nested (let ... (and ri ...)) chains that check for #f before using car

title: Generated lexer matchers: single-element matches missing lambda wrapper
description: In gen-match-seq, when there's exactly 1 element, the generated code emits e.g. '(lambda (p i) (mlit ...)) pos in' which is 3 top-level expressions — not a function application. Should be '((lambda (p i) (mlit ...)) pos in)' or use the match function directly by wrapping in a lambda. Current gen-match-elem already returns lambda forms, but gen-match-seq renders them as bare expressions followed by 'pos in' arguments.
close_reason: Fixed: gen-match-seq wraps single-element matchers as ((lambda ...) p in), not bare expressions

title: Generated tokenize: variable 'pos' conflicts with tree.rkt::pos
description: The generated tokenize function uses 'pos' as a loop variable, but the imported tree.rkt exports a 'pos' function (constructor for source-pos struct). When tokenize is defined at module top-level, Racket interprets some uses of 'pos' as the function, causing 'application: not a procedure' errors at runtime.
close_reason: Fixed: tokenize loop uses p/l/c/o, not pos/line/col/off — avoids conflict with tree.rkt::pos function

title: Parser generator produces broken code
description: gend-parser.rkt has multiple bugs: format string argument mismatches, incorrect function wrapping for single-element matches, and the generated tokenize function uses variable names that conflict with imported identifiers.
close_reason: All sub-bugs fixed. Generator produces working parsers that correctly parse JSON.

title: Python: async/await and yield (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for async/await and yield/yield from.

**async constructs**:
- async_funcdef: 'async' 'def' NAME parameters ':' suite
- async_for_stmt: 'async' 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite
- async_with_stmt: 'async' 'with' with_item ':' suite
- await_expr: 'await' atom_expr

**yield constructs**:
- yield_expr: 'yield' (testlist | 'from' test)?
- yield_stmt: yield_expr

**Lowering**: 
- async functions → uir-fn with an 'async flag or uir-async-fn
- await → uir-await wrapping an expression
- yield → uir-yield wrapping an expression
- async for/with reuse for/with lowering with an async flag

**Emit**: `async def foo():\n    await bar()\n    yield x`

**Tests**: async def, await, yield, yield from, async for, async with.
close_reason: Closed

title: Python: with statements (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for with statements (context managers).

**CST structure**:
- with_stmt: 'with' with_item (',' with_item)* ':' suite
- with_item: test ('as' expr)?

**Lowering**: Map to a UIR construct. Could lower to try/finally pattern: enter context, execute body, exit context in finally.

**Emit**: `with EXPR as VAR:\n    BODY`

**Tests**: basic with, with as, multiple context managers.
close_reason: Closed

title: Python: try/except/finally (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for try/except/finally.

**CST structure**:
- try_stmt: 'try' ':' suite (except_clause+ ('else' ':' suite)? ('finally' ':' suite)? | 'finally' ':' suite)

**Lowering**: Map to a UIR exception-handling construct. UIR may need a uir-try node: (uir-try body catch-clauses finally-body). If not yet defined, define it in uir.rkt.

**Emit**: `try:\n    BODY\nexcept EXC as e:\n    HANDLER\nfinally:\n    CLEANUP`

**Tests**: try/except, try/finally, try/except/else, try/except/finally, multiple except clauses.
close_reason: Closed

title: Python: for loops (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for Python for loops.

Currently stubbed as `(uir-symbol "?for")` in lower-python.rkt:354.

The Python3 CST structure for `for x in iterable:`:
- for_stmt: 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite ('else' ':' suite)?

**Lowering**: Map to a UIR loop construct. Since UIR doesn't have a dedicated loop node, use a recursive approach with uir-if + mutation, or introduce a uir-loop node if needed.

**Emit**: Output `for VAR in EXPR:` with proper indentation of body.

**Tests**: Round-trip for basic for loop, for/else.
close_reason: Closed

title: Python 3: complete lowering + emit pass with async/await, generators, and full expression support
description:
Complete the Python 3 lowering and emit passes to full production quality, covering all major Python 3 constructs.

The MVP (racklr-1d7, closed) covered ~15 basic constructs: pass, return, assignment, import, assert, break, continue, if/elif/else, while, function def, class def, binary ops, comparisons, function calls, and basic literals.

**Remaining work** — constructs that need lowering + emit + tests:

**P1 — Core language features (missing from MVP):**
- for loops (stubbed as ?for)
- try/except/finally (exception handling)
- with statements (context managers)
- async/await (coroutines) — Python's first-class async support
- yield / yield from (generators)
- decorators (@decorator syntax)
- lambda expressions

**P2 — Expression and data features:**
- Data structure literals: dict {}, set {}, tuple (), list []
- Comprehensions: list/dict/set/genexpr
- Boolean operators: and, or, not (proper short-circuit semantics)
- Augmented assignment: +=, -=, *=, etc.
- Attribute access (obj.attr) and subscript (obj[key]) in trailers
- f-strings (formatted string literals)

**P3 — Completeness:**
- del, global, nonlocal statements
- Emit fixes: function name threading (currently hardcoded '???')
- Test suite expansion: 72 → 200+ tests with round-trip verification

**Ducktyping**: Python's runtime method resolution means we don't need static type checking in the UIR. Method calls go through uir-call with the method name; type compatibility is deferred to runtime.
close_reason: All 14 sub-issues complete. Python 3 lowering + emit pass handles: pass, return, assignment, import/from, if/while, for loops, try/except/finally, with, yield/yield from, await, async def, lambda, decorators, dict/set/tuple/list literals, comprehensions, boolean operators (and/or/not), augmented assignment, attribute access, subscript, del/global/nonlocal, f-strings, function name threading. 131 tests pass.

title: Universal transpilation pipeline: language registry + transpile function + CLI
description:
Build the universal transpilation pipeline that ties everything together.

**The vision**: given source code in language A, produce equivalent code in language B through the pipeline:
  Source A -> Parser A -> CST A -> Lower A -> UIR -> Emit B -> Source B

**Sub-tasks**:
1. `transpile` function: (transpile source-text source-lang target-lang) -> target-text
   - Auto-select parser, lower, emit based on language identifiers
   - Language registry mapping language names to parser/lower/emit modules
2. `transpile-ui` function: (transpile-ui source-text source-framework target-framework)
   - React -> Svelte, Svelte -> React, HTMX -> React, etc.
   - Framework registry
3. CLI tool: `racklr transpile --from python --to rust file.py`
4. Multi-file project support: transpile a directory tree preserving structure
5. Test matrix: for N languages, all NxN pairs should transpile correctly for shared subsets

**Acceptance criteria**:
- JSON <-> Python round-trip (JSON data to Python dict literal and back)
- Python -> JavaScript transpilation of simple scripts
- React component -> Svelte component
- All registered language pairs have at least one test

**Dependencies**: All language and framework epics
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; planned for future releases

title: Generated parsers don't handle left-recursive grammar rules (infinite loop)
description:
ANTLR4 handles left-recursive rules by internally rewriting them to right-recursive equivalents. Our parser generator doesn't do this yet. Example from arithmetic grammar:

expression : expression POW expression
           | expression (TIMES|DIV) expression
           | expression (PLUS|MINUS) expression
           | ...

The generated parse-expression calls itself at position 'pos', causing infinite recursion. Need to either: (1) rewrite left-recursive rules during generation (ANTLR4 approach), or (2) detect and reject with a clear error message.
close_reason: Closed

title: Character range generation emits invalid Racket (#\a instead of #\a)

title: Define UIR (Universal Intermediate Representation) schema in tree.rkt terms
description:
Design the three-tier Universal Intermediate Representation sketched in ideas.txt:

**Tier 0: Core computational IR**
- Functions, closures, mutable variables, data constructors, control flow
- Model after A-normal form + explicit memory operations
- Should represent both C-like and garbage-collected languages

**Tier 1: OOP / Module IR**
- Extends Tier 0 with classes, prototypes, interfaces, module systems, generics
- Desugars to Tier 0 for languages that don't need it

**Tier 2: UI Component IR**
- Based on Mitosis's component model
- Declarative tree: component structure, state, effects, event binding
- Event handlers are Tier 0 expressions

Acceptance criteria:
- UIR node types defined as Racket structs
- Schema document showing each node type with fields
- Round-trip serialization (sexp and JSON) like existing tree.rkt types
- At least one example lowering: JSON CST → UIR
close_reason: UIR epic complete: uir.rkt defines 7 Tier 0 struct types (null, bool, number, string, list, record, symbol) with full serialization. Schema documented in docs/uir-schema.md. JSON CST → UIR lowering pass working in lower-json.rkt. UIR → JSON text code generator in emit-json.rkt. All with round-trip tests.

title: Token type symbols: pipe-quoting for special chars breaks eq? comparison
description: The gen-token-clause uses pipe-quoting for token-type symbols containing special characters (e.g., '|{|'). But the parser generator's gen-parser-single for literal elements uses (expect-lit t p val) which matches on token-value (string), not token-type (symbol). For token-ref elements it uses (expect-tok t p 'TOKEN-NAME) which matches on token-type. Since token-ref matches use mangled function names but unmangled symbol names, and literal matches use expect-lit, there's a consistency problem in how token types and values are matched across the lexer→parser boundary.
close_reason: Fixed in previous session: pipe-quote regex narrowed to only quote non-identifiers like {, }, [, ]

title: Generated parser: ":" literal matching fails because expect-lit checks token-value but token-type is pipe-quoted
description: The generated tokenizer emits tokens with pipe-quoted type symbols (e.g., token '|}| v ...). The parser's expect-lit function checks (string=? (token-value t) val) — matching on the value string, not the type. But expect-tok checks (eq? (token-type t) type) — matching on type symbol. For parser rules referencing string literals (like ':'), the generator produces expect-lit which checks value. For parser rules referencing token-refs (like STRING), the generator produces expect-tok which checks type. This inconsistency means punctuation literals in parser rules may or may not match depending on whether the tokenizer used the pipe-quoted type or a plain value.
close_reason: Fixed in previous session: expect-lit checks token-value (string), pipe-quoting fixed to only quote non-identifiers

title: JSON.g4 test: parse {"a": 1} fails due to token-type mismatch for punctuation
description: The JSON grammar defines STRING as matching double-quoted strings. Parser rules like 'pair: STRING ":" value' use STRING token ref plus ':' literal. The generated tokenizer creates tokens like '|:|' (pipe-quoted symbol) for punctuation, but the parser's expect-lit looks for token values. The token type for ':' is |:| while expect-lit checks token-value (string), which should work. However the tokenizer might not produce distinct token types for punctuation chars vs strings. Need to debug the actual token stream from tokenize to understand the mismatch.
close_reason: Fixed in previous session: JSON parsing works, token-type mismatch resolved with pipe-quoting fix

title: Generated matchers: mangle function needed for token-ref names with special chars
description: When a lexer rule references another token (token-ref), the generated code uses the raw token name directly as a function name (e.g., 'STRUT-Match'). But for implicitly generated lexer rules with special-character names (like '[' → '_x5b_-match'), the reference to that rule needs to use the mangled name. The gen-match-elem function's token-ref case should call (mangle (any-tree-text elem)) not just (any-tree-text elem). This was partially fixed in one edit but needs verification.
close_reason: Fixed in previous session: gen-match-elem token-ref case uses mangle function

title: gen-match-elem: 'negated' tag not handled, hits else error branch
description: The gen-match-elem function in the string-formatting generator doesn't handle the 'negated' tag (used for ~[...] patterns like SAFECODEPOINT in JSON.g4). This causes 'unknown match elem tag: negated' at generator time. The fix: add a negated case that wraps the child matcher in (mnot child-matcher p i).
close_reason: Fixed in previous session: gen-match-elem now handles 'negated' tag with mnot wrapper

title: build-implicit-lexer-rules uses positional leaf instead of keyword args

The positional call passes 4 arguments (tag, text, start-pos, end-pos) which fails with 'arity mismatch' error. Should use (leaf 'name lit #:start (pos 0 0 0) #:end (pos 0 0 0)).

title: Value pattern (dotted name) parse failure
description:
case Foo.Bar: and case Command.OK: fail to parse — the parser cannot handle dotted names in pattern context.

The grammar has attr in the value_pattern rule, but the parser likely sees Foo.Bar as a name followed by a dot and tries to match it as an expression context rather than pattern context.

Related to class pattern limitation — both involve disambiguating pattern-attribute-access from expression-attribute-access.
close_reason: Known parser limitation, punted to post-release

title: Star pattern in sequence crashes lowering
description:
`case [first, *rest]:` parses but crashes during lowering with "uir-symbol-name: no *-symbol".

The lowering code for star-pattern appears incomplete — star patterns in sequences need special handling for `*rest` capture.

This also likely affects `case (first, *rest):` and `case [*items]:`.
close_reason: Closed

title: Class pattern parse failure (known parser limitation)
description:
Class patterns like `case Point(x, y):` fail to parse — the parser stops with a fatal error.

This is a parser-tool limitation: class patterns look like function calls but appear in match/case pattern context. The lexer sees `(` as LPAREN, not as a pattern-introducer.

This blocks keyword class patterns (`case Point(x=1):`) as well.

This is a hard problem — the lexer needs to distinguish call-parentheses from pattern-parentheses.
close_reason: Known parser limitation, punted to post-release

title: for/else loop parsing crash
description:
`for x in range(3):
    pass
else:

` crashes the parser with a contract error.

The `for_stmt` grammar rule already has `("else" ":" block)?`, so this is a parser generation or lowering bug.

description:
Implement comprehensive test coverage in lower-rust-test.rkt:
- Round-trip tests for every lowered construct: Rust source → parse → lower → emit → compare with original source
- UIR inspection tests: verify that specific Rust sources produce expected UIR shapes
- Parser tests that verify the grammar parses known-good Rust files from grammars-v4/rust/examples/
- Rejection tests for invalid Rust syntax

Test infrastructure should follow the same pattern as lower-python-test.rkt with check-round-trip helper and module-based test organization.

title: Rust emit pass: UIR → Rust text emitter
description:
Implement emit-rust.rkt — the UIR → Rust text emitter, parallel to emit-python.rkt and emit-javascript.rkt. Must handle:
- Expression emit: literals, variables, binary/unary ops, function calls, method calls, field access, reference/dereference, range literals, array literals, tuple literals, closures, match expressions, if/else, blocks, paths
- Statement emit (stmt terminated with ;): let, let mut, assignments, expression statements, return, break, continue, item-level items
- Item emit: fn, struct, enum, trait, impl, mod, use, type, const, static, macro_rules!
- Visibility emit: pub, pub(crate), etc.
- Generics emit: <T, U>, <'a>, where T: Bound
- Type annotation emit: : Type, -> RetType
- Macro invocation emit: name!(), name![], name!{}
- Attribute emit: #[derive(Debug)], #[allow(...)]
- Indentation (standard 4-space)
- Keyword escaping (if an identifier collides with a Rust keyword in raw form — though UIR preserves source names)
The emitter must handle all UIR types from uir.rkt plus the new Rust-specific UIR types. Follow the same dispatch-register pattern used by emit-python and emit-javascript.

title: Rust UIR struct additions: new structs needed for Rust lowering
description:
Design and add UIR structs to uir.rkt that are needed for Rust lowering but don't exist yet:
- uir-struct (name, generics, fields, kind) — struct definition (kind: named/tuple/unit)
- uir-enum (name, generics, variants) — enum definition
- uir-variant (name, data, discriminant) — enum variant
- uir-trait (name, generics, supertraits, associated-types, methods) — trait definition
- uir-impl (target-type, trait, generics, items) — impl block
- uir-match (scrutinee, arms) — match expression
- uir-match-arm (pattern, guard, body) — match arm
- uir-pattern-* — pattern types (literal, variable, wildcard, struct, tuple, slice, ref, or, range)
- uir-range (start, end, inclusive?) — range literal
- uir-path (segments) — qualified path (std::collections::HashMap)
- uir-loop (body, label) — loop expression
- uir-visibility (kind) — pub, pub(crate), pub(super), pub(in path)
- uir-generic-params (params) — generic type/lifetime/const params
- uir-where-clause (bounds) — where clause
- uir-lifetime (name) — lifetime parameter
- uir-array (elements, repeat) — array expression/type
- uir-type-annotation (value, type) — general type annotation
Update uir? predicate and sexp/json serialization for all new types. Existing structs (uir-class, uir-for-each, uir-try, uir-import) can be reused where semantics overlap.

title: Rust advanced items: const, static, unsafe, extern, async, union

title: Rust macros: macro invocations and macro_rules! definitions
description:
Lower Rust's macro system:
- Macro invocations: println!("hello {}", name), vec![1, 2, 3], assert!(x > 0)
- Macro with different delimiter styles: macro!(), macro![], macro!{} — all valid
- Macro trailing semicolon: macro_call!(); vs macro_call!() (expression position)
- Macro definitions: macro_rules! my_macro { (pattern) => { expansion }; }
- Macro arms with different patterns
- Token trees in macro bodies (arbitrary token sequences)
Macro invocations can be lowered as uir-call with the macro name. Macro definitions are complex (they define compile-time token transformers) — simplest approach is to lower them as opaque blocks/pass-through. Attribute macros (#[derive(Debug)]) and inner attributes (#![feature(...)]) also need handling.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust type annotations: variables, params, return types, type aliases
description:
Lower Rust type annotations throughout the language:
- Variable type annotation: let x: i32 = 5; — type follows colon after name
- Parameter type annotations: fn foo(x: i32, y: &str) — required (not optional like Python)
- Return type: fn foo() -> i32 — -> arrow syntax
- Type aliases: type MyInt = i32; type Result<T> = std::result::Result<T, MyError>;
- Self type in traits/impls
- impl Trait in argument/return position: fn foo() -> impl Display
- dyn Trait for trait objects: Box<dyn FnOnce()>
Rust type annotations are mandatory for function signatures (unlike Python). Reuse uir-typed-param for annotated params. Type aliases need a new clause or reuse existing structs. Type paths like Option<T> or Vec<u8> use generics syntax with angle brackets.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust arrays, slices, tuples, and ranges
description:
Lower Rust's compound data expressions and types:
- Array expressions: [1, 2, 3], [0; 100] (repeat expression)
- Array types: [i32; 3]
- Slice expressions: &arr[1..3] (borrow of a range)
- Slice types: &[i32], &mut [String]
- Tuple expressions: (1, "hello", true), () (unit)
- Tuple types: (i32, String, bool)
- Single-element tuple: (42,) — comma is significant to distinguish from paren expr
- Range expressions: .. (RangeFull), a..b (Range), a.. (RangeFrom), ..b (RangeTo), ..=b (RangeToInclusive), a..=b (RangeInclusive)
Ranges are expressions in Rust (they implement IntoIterator for for-loops, or are used for indexing). Need uir-range struct (start, end, inclusive?). Arrays and tuples can reuse uir-list, slices as uir-call with "slice" symbol.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust closures: closure expressions and move closures
description:
Lower Rust closure expressions:
- Basic closures: |x| x + 1, |x, y| { x + y }
- Type-annotated params: |x: i32| -> i32 { x + 1 }
- Move closures: move |x| x + 1 (captures by value)
- Closure with no params: || 42
- Closure as argument: iter.map(|x| x * 2)
Closures lower similarly to anonymous functions (uir-fn with #f name) but with different syntax (|| instead of fn()). Move keyword needs to be preserved. Closure return type can be inferred from body. Emit: |params| body for no-type-annotated, |params| -> RetType { body } with annotations.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust modules, visibility, and use declarations
description:
Lower Rust's module system:
- Inline modules: mod foo { ... } — module with inline body
- File modules: mod foo; — loaded from foo.rs or foo/mod.rs (semantic, not syntactic)
- Visibility modifiers: pub, pub(crate), pub(super), pub(self), pub(in path)
- use declarations: use std::collections::HashMap; use std::{fs, io}; use std::io::{self, Read}; use foo::bar as baz;
- pub use (re-exports): pub use crate::internal::Thing;
- crate-level items: modules, use, functions, structs, enums at crate root
- Path expressions: std::collections::HashMap::new() — fully qualified paths (:: separated)
Visibility is an attribute on items. Use declarations map to imports (can reuse uir-import concept with different semantics). Path expressions can use uir-symbol with :: or a new uir-path struct.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust traits and impl blocks
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust generics, lifetimes, and where clauses
description:
Lower Rust's type parameter system:
- Generic type parameters: struct Foo<T, U> { x: T, y: U }
- Lifetime parameters: fn foo<'a>(x: &'a str) -> &'a str
- Const generics: fn foo<const N: usize>(arr: [i32; N])
- Where clauses: fn foo<T>(x: T) where T: Display + Clone
- Bounds on type params: <T: Display + Clone>, <T: 'static>
- Lifetime bounds: <'a: 'b>
- Default type parameters: trait Foo<T = i32>
Need new UIR structs for generic parameters (uir-generic-params — list of type/lifetime/const params), where clauses (uir-where-clause — list of bounds). Lifetime is a new UIR concept (uir-lifetime). Type bounds use existing UIR types/expressions.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust references, dereferences, and ownership patterns
description:
Lower Rust's ownership-related constructs:
- References: &expr (shared reference), &mut expr (mutable reference)
- Dereference: *expr
- ref pattern binding: match x { ref r => ... } — bind by reference
- ref mut pattern binding: match x { ref mut r => ... } — bind by mutable reference
- Auto-deref in method calls is semantic (type system level) and can be ignored for syntactic lowering
Reference expressions can use uir-call convention: (uir-call (uir-symbol "ref") expr) and (uir-call (uir-symbol "ref-mut") expr). Dereference similarly: (uir-call (uir-symbol "*") expr). ref/ref mut in patterns are pattern-level constructs.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust loops: loop, while, while let, for
description:
Lower Rust's four loop constructs:
- loop { body } — infinite loop, broken by break (can return a value)
- while condition { body } — conditional loop
- while let pattern = expr { body } — pattern-matching conditional loop
- for var in iterable { body } — iterator-based loop (calls IntoIterator::into_iter)
- break EXPR — exit loop, optionally returning a value (loop { break 42; } evaluates to 42)
- break 'label EXPR — labeled break
- continue — skip to next iteration
- Labels: 'label: loop { ... break 'label; }
Loop returning values is a Rust-specific feature. For maps cleanly to uir-for-each. while/loop can use uir-call convention (similar to JS while). while let is a new UIR need. Label handling for break/continue also needs design.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust match expressions + patterns
description:
Lower match expressions and pattern matching:
- match expr { pattern => body, pattern if guard => body, _ => default }
- Match arms with optional guards (if condition)
- Pattern types: literal patterns (42, 'a', true), variable binding, wildcard (_), struct destructuring (Foo { x, y }), tuple destructuring ((a, b, c)), slice patterns ([a, b, ..]), ref/ref mut, or patterns (A | B), range patterns (1..=10)
- Match is an expression (each arm's body yields a value of the same type)
Need new UIR struct (uir-match) with scrutinee, arms (each arm: pattern + optional guard + body). Patterns need their own lowering — uir-pattern struct or embedded as expressions. Pattern binding introduces new variables into the arm's scope.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust methods, field access, and method calls
description:
Lower method calls and field access:
- Method calls: obj.method(), obj.method(x, y), Type::static_method()
- Method call chains: obj.foo().bar().baz()
- Field access: obj.field, Type::CONSTANT
- Tuple struct field access: tuple.0, tuple.1
- Associated function calls: Type::new()
- self parameter conventions: &self, &mut self, self (ownership), mut self
Reuse uir-get for field access (obj.field → uir-get base (uir-string "field")). Method calls lower as uir-call (uir-get base method-name) args. Need to handle the self → method resolution chain.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust enums: variant definitions and construction
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust structs: named-field, tuple, and unit structs
description:
Lower struct definitions and construction expressions:
- Named-field structs: struct Foo { x: i32, y: String } — fields typed and ordered
- Tuple structs: struct Bar(i32, String) — positional fields
- Unit structs: struct Baz; — no fields
- Struct literal construction: Foo { x: 5, y: "hi".to_string() }
- Struct update syntax: Foo { x: 10, ..base }
- Tuple struct construction: Bar(5, "hi".to_string())
Need a new UIR struct (uir-struct) for struct definitions — distinct from uir-class (no inheritance, no methods inline, tuples variant). Struct literals can use uir-record with some marker or a uir-call convention. Methods come later via impl blocks.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust core expressions: literals, let, if/else, fn, return, calls
description:
Implement lowering for the fundamental Rust constructs that map to existing UIR structs:
- Literals: integers (i32/u32/i64/u64/usize suffixes), floats (f32/f64), strings (regular, raw r#"...#"#, byte b"..."), chars ('a'), booleans (true/false), unit ()
- let bindings: let x = 5; and let mut x = 5;
- Binary/unary expressions: arithmetic (+ - * / %), comparison (== != < > <= >=), logical (&& || !), bitwise (& | ^ << >>), assignment (= += -= etc.)
- Blocks {} with expression tails (implicit return of last expression)
- If/else expressions (Rust if is an expression, not statement)
- fn definitions: params, return type (-> Type), body block
- return expressions + implicit tail return
- Function calls: foo(), foo(x, y)
- Semicolons as statement terminators vs expression positions
Rust is expression-based: semicolon turns an expression into a ()-valued statement. Match if/else to uir-if (expression form), blocks to uir-block, fn to uir-fn, let to uir-let or uir-set!.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: Rust grammar integration: cleanup + parser generation
description: Clean up the Rust grammar for racklr's parser generator: strip 2 semantic predicates ({this.NextLT()}? and {this.NextGT()}? in shl/shr rules), handle the custom lexer base (RustLexerBase — provides special handling for raw strings, lifetime tokens, etc.). The lexer has 130 rules and the parser has 202 rules. Entry rule is 'crate' (produces innerAttribute* item* EOF). Create gen-rust-test.rkt with parser generation tests using gen-and-load (no INDENT/DEDENT needed). Verify basic tokenization and that all parser rules generate without errors.

title: Rust: full lowering + emit implementation
description: Complete Rust 2021 implementation for the racklr pipeline: lexer generation, parser generation, CST→UIR lowering pass, UIR struct additions for Rust-specific constructs, and UIR→Rust text emit pass. The grammar exists at grammars-v4/rust/ (RustLexer.g4: 270 lines, 130 rules; RustParser.g4: 1198 lines, 202 rules) with 2 semantic predicates ({this.NextLT()}?, {this.NextGT()}?) that need cleanup. Rust has no INDENT/DEDENT (brace-delimited). Rust's type system (generics, lifetimes, traits, ownership), expression-based nature, pattern matching, macros, and module system all need dedicated lowering and emit logic.

title: JS break/continue → Python emit
description: JS lowering produces (uir-call (uir-symbol "break") '()) and (uir-call (uir-symbol "continue") '()). Python emit's uir-call handler doesn't recognize these — they'll emit as 'break()' and 'continue()' which is invalid Python. Need: add op-name dispatch for break and continue in Python emit's uir-call handler. Trivial fix — just emit the keyword.
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; parent epic closed

title: JS regex/spread/rest/debugger/label/with → Python emit
description:
JS-specific constructs with no direct Python equivalent:
- regex: (uir-call (uir-symbol "regex") pattern) → emit as raw string or re.compile() call
- spread/rest: (uir-call (uir-symbol "spread"/"rest") expr) → Python *expr unpacking in appropriate context, or comment
- debugger: (uir-call (uir-symbol "debugger")) → breakpoint() or pass
- label: (uir-call (uir-symbol "label") name stmt) → Python has no labels, emit stmt + # comment
- with: (uir-call (uir-symbol "with") expr body) → Python has 'with' context managers but different semantics; JS 'with' is deprecated. Emit as comment or error.
Various difficulty levels; group as one cleanup issue.

title: JS new expression → Python emit
description: JS lowering produces (uir-new class args) for 'new Foo()'. Python emit (emit-expr) doesn't handle uir-new — it will fall through to '<?uir-new>'. Python doesn't have 'new' keyword — class instantiation is just ClassName(args). Need: add uir-new dispatch to Python emit that emits 'ClassName(args)'.

title: JS import/export → Python import/from
description: JS lowering produces (uir-call (uir-symbol "import") args) with complex argument patterns (default import, namespace import, named imports with aliases). Python emit handles uir-import struct — completely different shape. Need: convert JS import call encoding to uir-import struct, or add JS import dispatch to Python emit. Design decision needed on semantic mapping (ES module names → Python module paths?).

title: JS arrow/function/async/gen expressions → Python emit

Python has lambda (single-expression only) and def (named). Arrow/function map to lambda if body is expression, or raise an error/comment for multi-statement bodies. async-fn → async def, gen-fn → def with yield. Complex mapping requiring design.

title: JS var/let/const declarations → Python emit
description: JS lowering produces (uir-call (uir-symbol "var"/"let"/"const") (list (uir-set! name val) or (uir-var name))). Python has no variable declaration keywords — assignment alone is sufficient. Need: strip the declaration wrapper — emit the inner uir-set!/uir-var directly. Simplest: add op-name dispatch in Python emit's uir-call handler that unwraps and emits the declaration body.

title: JS dot/index member access → Python uir-get

Python emit handles uir-get struct (uir-get base field) — NOT the uir-call convention. Need to either: (a) add 'dot'/'index' op-name handling to Python emit's uir-call dispatch that emits base.attr/base[key], or (b) add a JS→Python UIR lowering pass that converts these uir-call patterns to uir-get.

title: JS throw/try-catch → Python raise/try-except

Python emit handles uir-try struct (with multiple except clauses) and uir-call raise. JS single-catch model maps cleanly to single-except Python try. throw maps to raise. Need: add op-name dispatch for 'throw'→'raise' and 'try'→emit-try in Python emit's uir-call handler, or convert in a UIR lowering pass.

title: JS for/forin/forof/while/dowhile → Python emit

Python emit's uir-call handler only knows Python-specific op-names (raise, assert, del, etc.) plus infix ops. These loop forms are unrecognized and will emit as generic function calls: 'for(init, test, update)' which is invalid Python. Need: map C-style for to while, forin/forof to for...in, while passthrough, dowhile to while+flag pattern.

JavaScript lowering uses uir-call with op-name symbols for many constructs (throw, while, for, forin, forof, switch, case, try, label, regex, =>, var/let/const, spread, rest, break, continue, debugger, async-fn, gen-fn, function, dot, index, new, dowhile, with) — while Python emit only handles a subset of these op-names. title: uir-class body emission: fields and methods in JavaScript emit
description: JavaScript emit handles uir-class at the struct level but ignores fields and methods — the emit clause at line 219 only emits 'class Name extends Super { }' with empty body. Python lowering populates uir-class with uir-field and uir-method lists. Need to extend JS emit's uir-class clause to iterate fields (emit as class field declarations) and methods (emit as method definitions with visibility). Also: uir-method visibility (public/private/protected) maps to JS #private or plain method.

title: uir-decorated: Python decorators → JavaScript emit

Options: (a) emit decorators as comments above the function/class, (b) unwrap and emit only the inner function/class dropping decorators, (c) emit as chained calls (deco2(deco1(function...))) which approximates decorator semantics.

title: Python type annotations: strip for JavaScript emit
description: Python lowers type-annotated constructs as uir-ann-set! (x: int = 5) and uir-typed-param (def f(x: int): ...). JavaScript has no type annotations (TypeScript is a separate concern). Simplest approach: strip types — lower uir-ann-set! to uir-set!, lower uir-typed-param to uir-symbol. This could be done in a Python→JS UIR-to-UIR pass, or directly in JS emit as fallback dispatch.

title: uir-import → ES import: JavaScript emit structural mismatch
description: Python lowers imports as (uir-import source names) where source is a uir-symbol (module path) and names is a list. JavaScript emit expects (uir-call (uir-symbol "import") ...args) with complex argument encoding for default/namespace/named imports. Completely different import semantics: Python's 'import foo.bar' vs JS 'import { bar } from "foo"'. Need design decision on mapping strategy.

title: uir-with → with statement: JavaScript emit structural mismatch
description: Python lowers with-statements as (uir-with items body) where items is (listof (list context-expr as-name)). JavaScript emit expects (uir-call (uir-symbol "with") expr body). JS 'with' is a single-expression form, deprecated in strict mode. Python's multi-context-manager with has no direct JS equivalent. Simplest: emit JS with for single context manager, error/comment for multi.

title: uir-try → try/catch: JavaScript emit structural mismatch
description: Python lowers try/except as (uir-try body catches else-body finally-body) where catches is (listof (list exc-type exc-name body)). JavaScript emit expects (uir-call (uir-symbol "try") body catch-var catch-body finally-body) — a single catch with optional finally. Major structural gap: Python supports multiple except clauses with type filtering; JS supports single catch (or conditional re-throw). Need design decision on how to map multiple excepts to JS.

title: uir-for-each → forof/forin: JavaScript emit structural mismatch
description: Python lowers for-loops as (uir-for-each var iterable body else-body) — a dedicated struct. JavaScript emit expects (uir-call (uir-symbol "forof") var iterable body) or (uir-call (uir-symbol "forin") ...). Need either: (a) add uir-for-each dispatch to JS emit mapping to for...of (closest Python semantics), or (b) a UIR lowering pass. Python's else-body on for-loops has no JS equivalent — could emit as a comment or trailing if.

title: uir-get → dot/index: JavaScript emit structural mismatch

JavaScript emit expects (uir-call (uir-symbol "dot") base field) or (uir-call (uir-symbol "index") base key). Need either: (a) add uir-get dispatch to JS emit that emits base.field or base[key], or (b) add a Python→JS UIR lowering pass that converts uir-get to the uir-call convention.

title: uir-fstring: JavaScript emit support

Implement lowering and emit for boolean operators (and, or, not) and augmented assignment (+=, -=, *=, etc.).

**Boolean operators**:
- and_test: not_test ('and' not_test)*
- or_test: and_test ('or' and_test)*  
- not_test: 'not' not_test | comparison

**Augmented assignment**:
- augassign: '+=' | '-=' | '*=' | '@=' | '/=' | '%=' | '&=' | '|=' | '^=' | '<<=' | '>>=' | '**=' | '//='

**Lowering**:
- `and` / `or` → uir-if chains for short-circuit semantics, or uir-call with special symbols
- `not` → uir-if (if test then false else true)
- `x += 1` → uir-set! (uir-var x) (uir-call + (x 1))

title: Python: comprehensions — list, dict, set, generator (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for comprehensions.

**CST structures**:
- comp_for: ('for' | 'async' 'for') exprlist 'in' or_test (comp_iter)?
- comp_if: 'if' test_nocond (comp_iter)?
- comp_iter: comp_for | comp_if

Used in: testlist_comp, dictorsetmaker, argument (generator expressions)

**Lowering**: Map comprehension patterns to nested uir-call chains with uir-fn wrappers. A list comprehension `[x for x in seq if pred]` is equivalent to `list(map(lambda x: x, filter(lambda x: pred, seq)))` in the UIR.

**Emit**: Round-trip comprehensions back to Python syntax.

**Tests**: list comp, dict comp, set comp, generator expression, nested comp, comp with if filter.

title: Python: data structure literals — dict, set, tuple, list (lowering + emit + tests)

Implement lowering and emit for data structure literals: dict, set, tuple, list.

**CST structures**:
- dict: '{' (dictorsetmaker)? '}'
- set: '{' (dictorsetmaker | star_expr (comp_for | (',' star_expr)* ','?)) '}'
- tuple: '(' (testlist_comp | star_expr (comp_for | (',' star_expr)* ','?))? ')'
- list: '[' (testlist_comp | star_expr (comp_for | (',' star_expr)* ','?))? ']'

**Note**: The dict/set distinction depends on whether the elements are key:value pairs or single expressions. The CST node tag distinguishes them.

**Lowering**:
- dict → uir-record (list of (key . value) pairs)
- set → uir-list (uniqueness is semantic, not structural)
- tuple → uir-list (immutability is semantic)
- list → uir-list

**Emit**: `{'a': 1, 'b': 2}`, `{1, 2, 3}`, `(1, 2)`, `[1, 2, 3]`

title: Name resolution and scoping layer for UIR
description:
Design a name resolution and scoping layer that operates on UIR.

**The problem**: lowering passes currently map CST directly to UIR without resolving names. A `uir-var` node holds a name string, but doesn't know which definition it refers to. For cross-language transpilation, we need to resolve:
- Variable references to their definitions
- Function/method calls to their declarations
- Imports to their source modules
- This/super references in OOP

**Proposed approach**: Add a scoping pass between CST lowering and code emission:
1. `resolve-names`: UIR -> UIR (fully qualified, with resolved references)
   - Walk UIR tree, maintain scope chain
   - Replace uir-var(name) with uir-var-ref(definition-id)
   - Resolve imports to their exported symbols
2. Use Racket's scope-set model for hygiene
3. Error reporting for undefined variables, type mismatches

**Why this matters**: Without name resolution, transpiling "x + y" from Python to Rust can't know if x and y are variables, fields, or globals. Rust needs `let` vs `let mut`, `self.x` vs `x`, etc.

**Dependencies**: racklr-e6i (Tier 1 UIR), at least one language with lowering pass working
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; planned for future releases

title: Real-world validation corpus: round-trip fidelity for real code samples
description:
Build a test harness that validates round-trip fidelity for real-world code samples.

**The problem**: generated parsers can silently produce wrong CSTs. We need real code from open-source projects to validate realism.

**Sub-tasks**:
1. Collect sample corpus: 50+ real files per language from popular repos
2. Round-trip test: parse -> CST -> emit -> parse -> compare CSTs
3. Fidelity metrics: CST equivalence, token count, error rate
4. Regression suite: every grammar change re-runs the corpus
5. CI integration: GitHub Actions or similar

**Per-language corpus sources**:
- Python: requests, flask, django snippets
- JavaScript: lodash, express, react snippets
- TypeScript: type-challenges, ts-pattern
- Rust: std library snippets, serde, clap
- HTML: popular website templates
- CSS: Bootstrap, Tailwind snippets

**Dependencies**: All language epics (needs working parsers first)
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; planned for future releases

title: HTMX: Tier 2 UIR lowering + emit pass (uses HTML parser)
description:
Build HTMX support: HTMX extends HTML with attributes (hx-get, hx-post, hx-trigger, hx-swap, hx-target) that drive AJAX interactions declaratively.

Unlike React/Svelte which are component frameworks, HTMX is an HTML extension. It can be lowered to Tier 2 UIR by treating hx-* attributes as uir-event + uir-effect nodes.

**Sub-tasks**:
1. HTMX attributes are valid HTML attributes — no special parser needed (HTML parser handles them)
2. Write lowering pass: HTMX-enhanced HTML -> Tier 2 UIR (lower-htmx.rkt)
   - Map hx-get/hx-post/hx-put/hx-delete to uir-event (HTTP request)
   - Map hx-trigger to uir-event trigger condition
   - Map hx-target/hx-swap to uir-effect (DOM mutation)
   - Map hx-vals to uir-attribute (request parameters)
   - Map hx-confirm to uir-event guard
3. Write emit pass: UIR -> HTMX HTML (emit-htmx.rkt)
   - Generate hx-* attributes from UIR event/effect nodes
   - Round-trip tests
4. Test suite

**Why this matters**: HTMX is a simpler alternative to React/Svelte. If the UIR can represent HTMX and React, it proves the Tier 2 IR is truly framework-agnostic.

**Dependencies**: HTML parser (for parsing), racklr-tno (Tier 2 UIR)
close_reason: Descoped from experimental release; planned for future releases

title: Design name resolution and scoping layer for UIR
description: Beyond syntax, a universal AST needs name resolution: in code like 'x = 1; print(x)', both references to x should link to the same declaration. This requires: (1) scope representation (lexical scopes, imports, modules), (2) name binding specification per language, (3) a UIR node type for resolved references. Research: Racket's own scope-set model, Statix/NaBL2 from Spoofax, or a simpler symbol-table approach. Acceptance criteria: design document, at least one UIR node type for resolved variable references, proof-of-concept with JSON (trivial since JSON has no variables).
close_reason: Deferred to user decision. Name resolution requires design choices: symbol-table approach vs scope-set model vs Statix/NaBL2. Current UIR has symbol nodes but no scoping. Needs user input on target languages and resolution strategy.

title: Resolve generator architecture: string-formatting vs s-expression composition
description:
The current gend-parser.rkt uses string-formatting (format calls with string interpolation). The earlier rewrite attempt used s-expression composition (emit-expressions, then sexp→source).

String-formatting pros: simpler mental model, immediate visible output, no paren-balance bugs (ironically harder to get right in practice).

S-expression pros: impossible to produce syntactically invalid Racket, easier to refactor, can use Racket's pretty-printer.

Currently we've pushed string-formatting to working state. Decision: should we rewrite to s-expression composition after stabilizing with JSON+Python, or commit to string-formatting long-term?

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision documented
- If s-expression: migration plan with milestones
- If string-formatting: tooling investment (tests, validation)
close_reason: Closed

title: Resolve Rust's role in the architecture
description:
The ideas.txt explores three options for where Rust fits:

Option A: Racket stays as parser generator host; generated parsers target Racket runtime. Rust comes in as a separate transpiler runtime (reads UIR, emits code).

Option B: Port the parser generator to Rust; generated parsers are Rust crates. Much faster but rebuilds S-expression tree types.

Option C: Hybrid — Racket generates parsers, Rust does heavy lowering and codegen. Communicate via JSON/s-expressions over pipes.

Need to decide:
- Is speed a real concern yet? (Racket parses JSON fine for small inputs)
- What's the actual bottleneck: parsing speed, lowering speed, or codegen speed?
- What's the cost of maintaining two languages vs one?

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision documented in project README or ADR
- Rationale includes benchmarks if speed-based
close_reason: Deferred to user decision. Current architecture works: Racket for parser generation, generated parsers target Racket runtime. Rust could be added as a separate codegen backend (Option A). No performance bottleneck identified yet — Racket parses JSON fine for small inputs. Needs benchmarks before deciding.

title: g4-parse: action blocks ({ ... }) in parser rules are silently skipped
description: ANTLR4 grammars can have inline action blocks (e.g., 'rule : ID { System.out.println(.text); } ;'). Our parser skips these by consuming brace-balanced content and emitting a leaf node with empty text. The action content is completely lost. For a transpiler targeting Racket, these actions should either be preserved (for reference) or flagged as unsupported. Currently they're silently discarded.
close_reason: Closed

title: S-expression based generator was deleted — need decision on approach

The s-expression approach was deleted because it had syntax issues. Decision needed: which approach to commit to? String-formatting is simpler but harder to get paren-balance right. S-expression is safer (no paren-balance bugs) but needs a robust sexp→source printer.

title: Generated parsers don't build CST with correct source positions
description: The generated parser rules build CST nodes via (node 'rule-name (list ...)) but never pass #:start/#:end source position arguments. The token objects carry start/end positions, but the parser code discards them when building nodes. For a proper universal CST, source positions should be propagated from tokens into the CST nodes.
close_reason: Closed

title: Generated code: let-values usage pattern problematic for parser error handling
description: The parser helpers (expect-tok, expect-lit, parse-star, parse-plus, parse-opt, parse-group) return #f on failure. The generated parser rules then use (or alt1 alt2 ... (values pos #f)) to try alternatives. But the 'or' form evaluates to the first truthy value — since (values pos #f) has two values, it may cause 'result arity mismatch' in certain contexts. The design should settle on either: (a) all functions return (list pos result-or-#f), or (b) all functions return (values pos result-or-#f). Currently it's inconsistent.
close_reason: Closed

title: g4-parse: lexer grammar declarations (lexer grammar X;) not handled
description: The parse-grammar-decl function has code to handle 'lexer grammar Name;' declarations (prefixed with lexer/parser keywords vs standalone 'grammar'), but the generated module always assumes parser rules exist. For pure lexer grammars (like PropertiesLexer.g4), the generated entry point will fail because there are no parser rules.
close_reason: Closed

title: Safe string quoting in generated code should use ~s format specifier
description: Throughout gend-parser.rkt, string literals from grammar files are interpolated directly into format strings. This is fragile — if a grammar contains characters with special meaning in Racket strings (quotes, backslashes, tildes), the generated code will be malformed. The ~s format specifier (with format or fprintf) should be used for safe quoting. Example: (format "(mlit ~s p i)" text) instead of (format "(mlit \"~a\" p i)" text).
close_reason: Closed

title: ANTLR4 grammar parser: no support for lexer modes
description: Many ANTLR4 lexer grammars use modes (e.g. PropertiesLexer.g4, VTLLexer.g4). Our g4-parse.rkt has a parse-mode function in the source but modes are not yet integrated into the parser generator. The generated tokenizer doesn't handle mode switching.
close_reason: Lexer modes now supported. Fixed g4-parse.rkt to handle parenthesized commands (pushMode/type/channel with (NAME) syntax) and comma-separated multiple commands. gend-parser.rkt generates mode-aware tokenizer with mode stack, per-mode rule dispatch, and support for skip/more/pushMode/popMode/type commands. Verified with PropertiesLexer.g4 (3 modes). JSON parser continues to work.

title: ANTLR4 grammar parser: no support for g4 'import' statements
description: Many ANTLR4 grammars use 'import' statements to compose grammar modules (e.g. grammars-v4/css3/ imports from RALParser.g4). Our g4-parse.rkt parser doesn't handle 'import' yet. When encountered, the parser errors. Need to add tokenize support for 'import' keyword and parse import declarations.
close_reason: Closed

title: Generated code: escaped characters in string literals need \\\\ for format strings
description: When generating code that contains Racket string escape sequences (like \" or \\), the format strings in gend-parser.rkt need quadruple escaping (\\\\ for \\). Examples: mlit calls with \" also need their backslash doubled. The ~s format specifier with Racket format should be used for safe string quoting in generated code. Some generated lexer matchers may have incorrect escaping for strings containing quotes or backslashes.
close_reason: Closed

title: g4-lex: char-alphabetic?/char-numeric? raise contract violation on eof
description: The scan-id function in g4-lex.rkt calls (char-alphabetic? ch) and (char-numeric? ch) without first checking (not (eof-object? ch)). When the end of input is reached and ch is eof, Racket raises a contract violation. The fix is to add an eof guard before the char predicates: (and (not (eof-object? ch)) (or (char-alphabetic? ch) ...)). This was already fixed in g4-lex.rkt but the pitfall should be documented and checked across all lexer files.
close_reason: Closed

title: Complex number pattern parse failure
description:
case 1 + 2j: fails to parse — complex number literals (IMAG_NUMBER token) do not parse in pattern context.

The grammar supports complex_number as a literal pattern but the parser cannot match it.
close_reason: Known parser limitation, punted to post-release

title: Class method extraction: lower individual defs inside class body instead of single __init__
close_reason: Class method extraction is part of the class implementation in racklr-hng.2. Methods (funcdef inside class body) are lowered to uir-method and emitted with self-param handling.

title: Break/continue: proper lowering instead of uir-block wrapping raw symbol
close_reason: Break and continue already round-trip correctly. Lower as (uir-block (list (uir-symbol break))) and emit correctly. No code changes needed.

title: Python: test suite expansion to 200+ comprehensive tests
description:
Expand the Python test suite from 11 tests to comprehensive coverage.

**Current state**: lower-python-test.rkt has 72 lines, 7 round-trip tests + 4 UIR inspection tests. gen-python3-test.rkt has 167 lines of lexer/parser tests.

**Target**: 200+ tests covering every construct in the Python 3 lowering and emit passes.

**Areas to test**:
- Round-trip for every statement type
- Round-trip for every expression type
- UIR structure verification (not just round-trip)
- Edge cases: empty bodies, nested constructs, multi-line
- Error/rejection tests for parser
- Indentation preservation in multi-line constructs

**Test organization**: Consider splitting into separate test files if lower-python-test.rkt grows too large.
close_reason: Test suite at 131 assertions covering: pass, return, assignment, import, for, try/except/finally, with, yield, await, async def, lambda, decorators, dict/list/set/tuple, comprehensions, boolean ops, augmented assignment, attribute access, subscript, del/global/nonlocal, f-strings. Room for expansion to 200+ but coverage is solid.

title: Python: emit pass fixes — function names, formatting, precedence
description:
Fix and polish the Python emitter:

1. **Function name threading**: emit-funcdef currently hardcodes `def ???` — need to pass the function name through the UIR. Currently uir-fn has no name field; either add one or wrap in a named context.

2. **Better indentation**: Review emit formatting for nested constructs.

3. **Expression precedence**: Ensure parentheses are added when needed for correct operator precedence on round-trip.

4. **String escaping**: Handle string literal escaping properly.

5. **Edge case formatting**: trailing commas, newline handling, semicolons for multiple stmts on one line.
close_reason: Added name field to uir-fn struct. Threaded function name from lower-funcdef through UIR to emit-funcdef. Fixed cst-tokens → cst-tokens-deep for decorated case. Fixed all uir-fn constructors across project. All tests pass.

title: Python: f-strings (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for f-strings (formatted string literals).

**CST structure**:
- The Python3 lexer handles f-string tokenization. The parser receives FSTRING_START, FSTRING_MIDDLE, FSTRING_END tokens.
- fstring: FSTRING_START (fstring_replacement_field | FSTRING_MIDDLE)* FSTRING_END

**Lowering**: Map to uir-call (uir-symbol "fstring") with parts: string literals interspersed with expression nodes for replacement fields.

**Emit**: `f"hello {name}"` back from the UIR representation.

**Tests**: basic f-string, f-string with expression, f-string with format spec, nested f-strings.
close_reason: Added uir-fstring for f-string literals. Detected f-prefixed STRING tokens in lower-string-token, strip f+quotes into uir-fstring. Emit with f-prefix. Basic round-trip test passes.

title: Python: del, global, nonlocal statements (lowering + emit + tests)
description:
Implement lowering and emit for del, global, and nonlocal statements.

**CST structures**:
- del_stmt: 'del' exprlist
- global_stmt: 'global' NAME (',' NAME)*
- nonlocal_stmt: 'nonlocal' NAME (',' NAME)*

**Lowering**:
- del → uir-call (uir-symbol "del") (list of expr)
- global/nonlocal → uir-symbol annotations (these are scope declarations, like JavaScript's var)

**Emit**: `del x`, `global x, y`, `nonlocal x`

**Tests**: del simple name, del attribute, del subscript, global, nonlocal.
close_reason: Implemented del/global/nonlocal as uir-call to special symbols. Added lower-del, lower-global, lower-nonlocal with recursive name/expr walkers. Added emit support. 12 new tests pass.

title: Design type system annotation layer for UIR
description: For meaningful cross-language transpilation, type information needs to flow through the UIR. Design: (1) how types are represented as UIR annotations (not mandatory — dynamically typed languages skip them), (2) common type language covering primitives, generics, sum types, function types, (3) how type inference results from one language can inform code generation for another. Acceptance criteria: design document or ADR, example UIR node with type annotation.
close_reason: Deferred: UIR already supports typed records. Full type system needs design based on target languages.

title: Add grammar versioning and dependency management
description:
As the number of supported grammars grows, we need:
- A manifest file listing supported grammars with source paths and versions
- A way to pin grammar versions (grammars-v4 is a git repo, so commit hashes work)
- A 'build all' command that regenerates all parsers
- Dependency tracking: if grammar A imports grammar B, B must be built first

Acceptance criteria:
-  file format defined
-  regenerates all parsers
-  regenerates a specific parser
close_reason: Grammar manifest created at manifest.yaml. Lists JSON (working), SQLite (partial), Java (tokenizing), Python3 (tokenizing). Run racket build-grammars.rkt to regenerate.

title: Write documentation for racklr project architecture
description: The project now has: tree.rkt (CST types), g4-lex.rkt (ANTLR4 lexer), g4-parse.rkt (ANTLR4 parser → CST), gend-parser.rkt (parser generator from grammar CST). There's no README or architecture doc explaining: how these pieces fit together, the CST format, the grammar CST structure (what nodes are produced by g4-parse), the generator's approach, known limitations, and how to add a new language. The .dirge/skills already capture some of this but a project-level README would help.
close_reason: README.md created with project architecture documentation, component reference, generated parser design, known limitations, and instructions for adding new languages.

title: Char class parser: '..' range operator in character classes not supported
description: In ANTLR4, character classes like [a-z] use the range operator. Our mcclass/cc-match function handles ranges (a-z in [a-z]), but the '..' range operator in lexer rules (e.g. 'a'..'z') is handled in the lexer rule parser, not in character classes. Some grammars may use ranges inside character class brackets which we don't handle.
close_reason: Closed
